channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Best and Worst Moments From the 2026 Tony Awards: Pink, Maya Rudolph, Cole Escola, ‘Cats’
item.description: Pink brought sincerity and high energy as the host, musical numbers by “Cats,” “Ragtime” and “Chicago” popped, and Maya Rudolph and Cole Escola mined comic gold.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: Pink, center, flanked by Megan Thee Stallion, Neil Patrick Harris and other performers during the opening number at the Tony Awards.

item.title: Was Scott Rudin the Biggest Winner at This Year’s Tony Awards?
item.description: Scott Rudin is a lead producer of “Death of a Salesman,” but he kept a low profile this awards season after a four-year hiatus prompted by bullying allegations.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.category: What We Did Before Our Moth Days (Play)
item.media:description: Nathan Lane, the star of “Death of a Salesman,” accepted the Tony Award for best play revival surrounded by cast members and co-producers. Conspicuously not present: the lead producer Scott Rudin.

item.title: On Broadway’s Biggest Night, ‘Saturday Night Live’ Shows Its Strength
item.description: The long-running sketch comedy show proved its power during the Tony Awards on Sunday, with Lorne Michaels and several former stars grabbing the spotlight.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor and Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:description: Lorne Michaels, the creator of “Saturday Night Live,” accepting the Tony Award for best new musical on Sunday.

item.title: ‘Backrooms’ and ‘Obsession’: The Lessons Behind These Horror Hits
item.description: Two Times critics unpack the twin phenomenon of “Backrooms” and “Obsession” and what lessons should — and shouldn’t — be learned from their massive success.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson and Jason Zinoman

item.title: Bounty of Frida Kahlo Art Emerges in Mexico’s Restored Museum
item.description: Diego Rivera’s great patron Dolores Olmedo regarded Frida as a rival but bought 26 of her artworks, the largest collection. Their work is on view in Mexico City.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Burnett and Pablo López Luz
item.media:credit: Banco de México Diego Rivera Frida Kahlo Museums Trust, Mexico, D.F./Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York
item.media:description: Frida Kahlo, “Autorretrato con Changuito (Self-Portrait With Monkey),” 1945 at the reopened Dolores Olmedo Museum in Mexico City.

item.title: Tony Award Winners 2026: See the Full List
item.description: The Tony Awards were held at Radio City Music Hall in New York City.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times
item.media:description: John Lithgow, the star of “Giant,” with his award for best actor in a play.

item.title: The One Tony Award Our Readers Would Have Changed
item.description: Thousands of people contributed their Broadway favorites of the season ahead of the Tony Awards. Here’s how their votes stacked up.
item.dc:creator: Robin Kawakami
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times; Photographs by Sara Krulwich/The New York Times

item.title: Our Favorite Photos From the Tony Awards
item.description: As Broadway’s brightest gathered for the industry’s biggest night, here’s an up-close look at what you couldn’t see on TV.
item.dc:creator: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet
item.media:description: Lesley Manville after she won for her performance in “Oedipus” on Sunday night.

item.title: Tonys 2026 Highlights: ‘Schmigadoon!’ and ‘Death of a Salesman’ Are Big Winners
item.description: “Ragtime” won best musical revival, while “Death of a Salesman” led with six prizes. Joshua Henry, Caissie Levy, John Lithgow and Laurie Metcalf were among the winners.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: The New York Hip-Hop Soundtracking the Knicks’ Run to the N.B.A. Finals
item.description: From old-school stars to young upstarts, the birthplace of hip-hop is basking in the big moment.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:description: The rapper Fat Joe has been one of the most visible supporters of the Knicks on their playoff run.

item.title: ‘Every Year After’ and 8 More Shows to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: The adaptation of a Carley Fortune novel airs and a new season of “Below Deck Mediterranean” begins.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez

item.title: Barry Manilow Can’t Smile Without Fans’ Help on This Song
item.description: The singer has bigger hits but “Can’t Smile Without You” has become a personal touchstone for fans who have helped him perform it live.
item.dc:creator: Rob Tannenbaum
item.media:description: Manilow, 82, is slated to resume his “farewell tour” later this month after postponing dates amid a bout with lung cancer.

item.title: ‘Scary Movie’ and the Art of the Pop Culture Gag
item.description: From “Saw” traps with Dr. Phil to TikTok-era tropes, we catalog the gags, goofs and groan-worthy bits from the “Scary Movie” universe.
item.dc:creator: Kellina Moore
item.media:description: Marlon Wayans in a scene from “Scary Movie 6” recalling the 2017 horror film “Get Out.”

item.title: Late Night Roasts Trump for Walking Out on ‘Meet the Press’
item.description: Jon Stewart called the president’s interview his “worst nightmare: a woman who won’t stop asking pertinent questions.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)

item.title: Maximalism Is Back at the Tonys
item.description: Our chief theater critic, Helen Shaw, shares her highlights of the Tony Awards on Sunday in New York City.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw, Léo Hamelin and Laura Salaberry

item.title: 2026 Tonys: How to Get Tickets for Winning Plays and Musicals
item.description: Many of the winning plays and musicals are still onstage, and some are closing soon. Here’s a guide to help you navigate the field and find tickets.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes and Rachel Sherman
item.media:description: A scene from “Schmigadoon!,” which won four Tony Awards.

item.title: Patrick Bruel, French Singer, Is Taken Into Custody Over Sex Assault Claims
item.description: Mr. Bruel, long a beloved celebrity in France, has denied allegations raised by 13 women dating to 1997.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:description: The French singer and actor Patrick Bruel in Paris last year.

item.title: Gordon S. Wood, Pioneering Historian of Early America, Dies at 92
item.description: In a Pulitzer-winning book, “The Radicalism of the American Revolution,” he wrote that the colonists rose up against an entire worldview, not just against taxation.
item.dc:creator: David Stout
item.category: The Radicalism of the American Revolution (Book)
Power and Liberty: Constitutionalism in the American Revolution (Book)
The 1619 Project: A New Origin Story (Book)
item.media:credit: Brooks Kraft LLC/Corbis, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Gordon S. Wood was awarded the National Humanities Medal by President Barack Obama in 2011. He worked to deepen understanding of the forces and events that led to the birth of the United States.

item.title: At the Tony Awards After-Parties, a ‘Harry Potter’ Reunion and Winner Celebrations
item.description: A crowd that included Daniel Radcliffe, Cole Escola and Carrie Coon celebrated with whiskey ice cream at Rockefeller Center and show tunes at the Carlyle hotel.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr, Nancy Coleman, Nina Westervelt and Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet
item.category: Harry Potter and the Cursed Child (Play)
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Megan Thee Stallion dancing to a song performed by Solea Pfeiffer at the Tony Awards after-party at the Carlyle hotel in Manhattan on Sunday.

item.title: B.F.I. Preserves ‘Charlie Bit My Finger’ and 430 Videos in Archive of Viral Moments
item.description: The British Film Institute assembled the memes in an archive of culturally significant internet moments. See what made the cut.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands

item.title: At the Tonys, calls in support of trans rights and free speech.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor

item.title: ‘Schmigadoon!’ Wins Best Musical Tony Award
item.description: The knowing, and loving, musical theater satire won the coveted award of the night.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: A scene from Broadway’s “Schmigadoon!,” which is running at the Nederlander Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Joshua Henry Wins the Tony for Best Actor in a Musical.
item.description: As Coalhouse Walker Jr. in “Ragtime,” Henry belts out emotional solos that have prompted mid-show standing ovations.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman
item.media:credit: Vincent Tullo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Joshua Henry, who plays Coalhouse Walker Jr. in the revival of “Ragtime.”

item.title: Caissie Levy wins the Tony for best actress in a musical.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:description: From left: Joshua Henry, Caissie Levy and Brandon Uranowitz in “Ragtime.”

item.title: ‘Death of a Salesman’ Wins the Tony for Best Revival of a Play
item.description: Arthur Miller’s classic won the same award in 1984, 1999 and 2012.
item.dc:creator: Jesse Green
item.media:description: Laurie Metcalf, left, as Linda and Nathan Lane as Willy Loman in the Arthur Miller play “Death of a Salesman” at the Winter Garden Theater in New York.

item.title: ‘Ragtime’ Wins the Tony for Best Revival of a Musical
item.description: Based on the novel by E.L. Doctorow, “Ragtime” follows three communities colliding in turn-of-the-20th-century New York.
item.dc:creator: Jesse Green
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: Nichelle Lewis, left, as Sarah and Joshua Henry as Coalhouse Walker Jr. in the musical “Ragtime.”

item.title: Lesley Manville wins the Tony for best actress in a play.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:description: Lesley Manville and Mark Strong in “Oedipus.”

item.title: ‘Liberation’ Wins the Tony for Best Play
item.description: The playwright Bess Wohl is the first American woman to win this award since Wendy Wasserstein won for “The Heidi Chronicles” in 1989.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski
item.media:description: From left: Audrey Corsa, Susannah Flood, Irene Sofia Lucio and Adina Verson in “Liberation.”

item.title: June Squibb is one of the oldest actors ever to be nominated for a Tony.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: June Squibb in the play “Marjorie Prime,” which had a limited run last winter at the Helen Hayes Theater.

item.title: John Lithgow Wins the Tony for Best Actor in a Play
item.description: In “Giant,” Lithgow leans into monstrosity to play the famed author Roald Dahl.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman
item.media:description: John Lithgow as Roald Dahl in “Giant” at the Music Box Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: A Landmark Tonys Win for the Trans Costume Designer Qween Jean
item.description: Qween Jean won for “Cats: The Jellicle Ball,” becoming the first openly transgender person to win a Tony Award, according to a publicist for the show.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: Qween Jean accepting the Tony Award for best costume design of a musical, for “Cats: The Jellicle Ball.”

item.title: Pink Is Hosting Tonight’s Tony Awards
item.description: The pop star, a former theater kid, will also perform on Sunday. Her theater-loving daughter approves of the gig.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pink arrives at the 79th Tony Awards before hosting the ceremony.

item.title: A middle-school teacher who’s sent students to Broadway wins excellence in education award.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Vinny Varsalona/Vinny Varsalona, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Freddie Hendricks, a teacher at Utopian Academy for the Arts in Ellenwood, Ga., also founded the Youth Ensemble of Atlanta.

item.title: Who is going to win tonight?
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson

item.title: American Players Theater is receiving the regional theater award.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:credit: Eric Ruby for The New York Times
item.media:description: Samantha Newcomb and Briana J. Resa, top center on the balcony, in a 2024 American Players Theater production of “Much Ado About Nothing.”

item.title: André Bishop, Jules Fisher and James Lapine to receive lifetime achievement awards.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes
item.media:credit: Dolly Faibyshev for The New York Times, Jenny Anderson/Getty Images For Tony Awards Pro, Jemal Countess/Getty Images For Tony Award Prod
item.media:description: From left, the producer André Bishop, the lighting designer Jules Fisher and the book writer and director James Lapine.

item.title: 38 Tony nominees on the strangest skills they’ve picked up for a show.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson

item.title: What’s onstage this summer?
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Evan Jenkins for The New York Times
item.media:description: A play inspired by the horror franchise “Paranormal Activity” makes its Broadway bow in August.

item.title: Tonys 2026 Takeaways: ‘Schmigadoon!’ and ‘Death of a Salesman’ Are Big Winners
item.description: The Broadway revival of “Salesman” led with six Tony Awards. “Schmigadoon!” won best new musical, and “Ragtime” and “Liberation” also took home major prizes.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson

item.title: Here’s what to expect at tonight’s ceremony.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: The CBS telecast of the Tonys introduces the latest plays and musicals to a television audience of several million.

item.title: How to watch the Tony Awards.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: Pink will host the 79th Tony Awards at Radio City Music Hall. The ceremony, which will be broadcast live on CBS and is available to stream on Paramount+, begins at 8 p.m. Eastern.

item.title: Tribeca Festival Denounces Pair Who Joked on the Red Carpet About Rape
item.description: A clip of the actor Elon Gold with Lizzy Savetsky, a social media influencer, at the premiere of “The Wedding Entertainer” has been circulating online.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses and Johnny Diaz
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times; Rob Kim, via Getty Images for Tribeca Festival

item.title: ‘Scary Movie’ Returns Politically Incorrect Humor to Top of the Box Office
item.description: The film took in $55 million over the weekend in the United States and Canada. It was the biggest opening for an R-rated comedy in 12 years.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: ‘Earth, Wind & Fire’ Review: Questlove’s Tribute to an Inspiration
item.description: In “To Be Celestial vs. That’s the Weight of the World,” he draws a compelling portrait of Maurice White, the band’s founder, and his enduring legacy.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny
item.category: Earth, Wind & Fire: To Be Celestial vs That's the Weight of the World (Movie)

item.title: 5 Takeaways From Scott Pelley’s Interview With The New York Times
item.description: Mr. Pelley, who was at CBS News for 37 years, including as a White House correspondent and a “60 Minutes” correspondent, spoke in his first extended interview since he was fired.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:description: In his first sit-down interview since he was fired from CBS News, Scott Pelley talks about his recent experiences at the network.

item.title: Carter Brey, New York Philharmonic Cellist, Retires
item.description: Carter Brey, the New York Philharmonic’s principal cellist for the last 30 years, is retiring from the orchestra at the end of this season.
item.dc:creator: Anastasia Tsioulcas and James Estrin
item.media:description: During recent bows, Brey acknowledged the audience’s standing ovation then lithely maneuvered himself out of the spotlight.

item.title: June Squibb, the ‘Foulest Mouth on Broadway’, Is Up for a Tony at 96
item.description: At 96, June Squibb is one of the oldest acting nominees in the history of the Tony Awards. Back in the 1960s, she was known for an entirely different distinction.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: June Squibb has received her first Tony nomination, for her featured role in “Marjorie Prime.”

item.title: What Steven Spielberg Taught Me About Fear, Catharsis, and Being Human
item.description: Hollywood is struggling, but Spielberg insists that the big screen is still the best place to work out our collective dreams, joys and sorrows.
item.dc:creator: Wesley Morris
item.media:credit: Paolo Pellegrin/Magnum, for The New York Times
item.media:description: “For years, I was working out my mom and dad’s divorce through my stories,” Spielberg says.

item.title: How to Watch the 2026 Tony Awards
item.description: The ceremony, at Radio City Music Hall, will be broadcast on CBS starting at 8 p.m. Eastern, and livestreamed on Paramount+ for premium subscribers.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: Pink will host the 79th Tony Awards at Radio City Music Hall. The ceremony, which will be broadcast live on CBS and is available to stream on Paramount+, begins at 8 p.m. Eastern.

item.title: Americans Love Soccer Stars Who Aren’t Made in America
item.description: The United States has gone wild over foreign-born players on several occasions. Now the largest World Cup ever is arriving.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:description: The Argentine star Lionel Messi is about to play in his sixth World Cup. His pink Inter Miami jersey became a hot item when he began playing in the United States in 2023.

item.title: Despite Protest, Ye Takes the Stage for Thousands of European Fans
item.description: Nearly 40,000 fans came out to hear the rapper formerly known as Kanye West in the Netherlands on Saturday, even as other European countries had canceled his concerts.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Mouneb Taim/Associated Press, via Ap Photo/Mouneb Taim
item.media:description: “This crowd is too crazy,” Ye said inside the GelreDome stadium in Arnhem, the Netherlands, on Saturday.

item.title: Kennedy Center Loses Case Against Musician Who Canceled Over Trump Naming Dispute
item.description: A judge sided with jazz performer Chuck Redd, who canceled a 2025 holiday concert after President Trump’s name was added to the building.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: A judge threw out a lawsuit filed by the John F. Kennedy Center against a jazz musician who canceled a Christmas Eve performance.

item.title: An Edith Wharton Short Story Is Published About 100 Years Later
item.description: The short story, which is set during World War I, is believed to have been printed for the first time on Friday. The story is thought to have been written no earlier than July 1918.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library
item.media:description: Edith Wharton in France in 1923. Famous for her sharp satires of New York society in the Gilded Age, Wharton spent World War I working on the front lines of French relief efforts.

item.title: Madonna Premieres ‘Confessions II,’ a Star-Studded Short Film, at Tribeca Film Festival
item.description: “Confessions II” features songs from the pop star’s coming album of the same name. At one point, green lasers shoot from the pelvises of gyrating women.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:description: Madonna during a surprise concert in Times Square on Thursday. “I came to this earth to be a doer, not a watcher,” she said after her new film premiered on Friday.

item.title: Taylor Swift, Madonna: 9 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: Taylor Swift is back with a “Toy Story” contribution while Madonna returns to the dance floor.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:description: Madonna performed new songs, including “Love Sensation,” at a surprise concert in Times Square in New York on Thursday.
